revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Noro (priestess)]][[fr:Noro (prêtresse)]][[ja:ノロ]][[ko:노로]][[pt:Noro]][[zh:祝女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Noro (priestess)]][[fr:Noro (prêtresse)]][[ja:ノロ]][[ko:노로]][[pt:Noro]][[zh:祝女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Noro (priestess)]][[fr:Noro (prêtresse)]][[ja:ノロ]][[ko:노로]][[pt:Noro]][[zh:祝女]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11325791]] Wikidata name: noro [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Priestess of the Ryukyuan religion}} {{italic title}} [[File:Noro.JPG|thumb|right|A {{transliteration|ja|noro}} priestess in traditional clothes]] {{transliteration|ja|'''Noro'''}} ({{lang|ja|祝女}}, sometimes {{lang|ja|神女}} or {{lang|ja|巫女}}) ({{langx|ryu|ヌール|nuuru}})<ref>[http://ryukyu-lang.lib.u-ryukyu.ac.jp/srnh/details.php?ID=SN40789 ヌール] 首里・那覇方言データベース</ref> are {{ill|Priest|en|Priest|WD=Q42603|lt=Priest}}esses of the [[Ryukyuan religion]] at [[Utaki]]. They have existed since at least the beginning of the [[History of the Ryukyu Islands#Gusuku period|Gusuku period]] (late 12th century) and continue to perform rituals even today. They are distinct from {{transliteration|ryu|{{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=yuta}}}} (psychics), but are classified as {{transliteration|ryu|kaminchu}} ("godly people").
==History== According to the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Chūzan Seikan|en|Chūzan Seikan|WD=Q8015936|lt=Chūzan Seikan}}}} and {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Chūzan Seifu|en|Chūzan Seifu|WD=Q10875195|lt=Chūzan Seifu}}}}, the first {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were the daughters of Tentei-shi, who was a descendant of the creation goddess, [[Amamikyu]]. The first daughter became the first {{nihongo|royal priestess|聞得大君|{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi}}}} ({{transliteration|ryu|chifi-ufujin}}), and the second daughter became the first village priestess ({{transliteration|ja|noro}}).<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The god of fire gave a piece of fire from [[Ryūgū-jō]] to each {{transliteration|ja|noro}} to create a village hearth, from which each family in the village would take fire to maintain their own family hearths.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} maintained the royal hearth. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were charged with conducting official rituals and ceremonies for their respective village. The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} was charged with conducting rituals and ceremonies on behalf of the entire kingdom, and traveled with the king to [[Sefa-utaki]] to worship Amamikyu.
Upon taking the throne in 1469, King {{ill|Shō En|en|Shō En|WD=Q1074326|lt=Shō En}} made his sister the Chief {{transliteration|ja|noro}} of his home of {{ill|Izena, Okinawa|en|Izena, Okinawa|WD=Q981332|lt=Izena}}, and his daughter {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 103-104.</ref> During the reign of {{ill|Shō Shin|en|Shō Shin|WD=Q281765|lt=Shō Shin}} ({{reign|1477|1526}}), the priestess system was centralized under the {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}'s authority and a {{transliteration|ja|noro}} was assigned to every village in the kingdom.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} also had a new residence built near {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle}} so she could maintain the [[Sonohyan-utaki]].<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 111.</ref>
After the 1609 [[Invasion of Ryukyu|invasion by Satsuma]], Confucian thought entered the Ryukyuan government and began eroding the authority of the priestesses. Satsuma placed a demand on the Ryukyuan government that women were forbidden to own land; however, the government ignored this demand in respect to the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. [[Shō Shōken]], acting as Prime Minister of Ryukyu, convinced the king to stop or scale down a number of religious rituals and ceremonies in the name of saving money and minimizing aspects of Ryukyuan culture that might seem "backwards" to Satsuma, such as the king and {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}'s pilgrimage to Sefa-utaki. However, local village priestesses still retained influence and power until the end of the 19th century.
After Japan annexed the Ryukyu Kingdom in 1879, the Meiji government began attempts at absorbing the Ryukyuan religion into {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}. The priestesses and their shrines were co-opted by the government and registered. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were prohibited from being recognized as part of the Shinto priesthood and, unlike their Shinto counterparts, were not guaranteed protection by the state. Furthermore, the government abolished their salaries. While the government was unable to confiscate {{transliteration|ja|noro}} land, in 1910 their land were commuted to monetary stipends which were then limited in use to exclude personal expense, leaving the {{transliteration|ja|noro}} with no government income. Then-Governor of Okinawa Prefecture [[:en:Hibi Shigeaki|Hibi Shigeaki]] stated that this limitation on the use of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} land stipends was "for the maintenance of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} lands [...] for their eventual reclassification into [Shinto] shrines."<ref>Tze May Loo, ''Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan'' (New York: Lexington Books, 2014), 102.</ref> As the influence of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} declined, the popularity of {{transliteration|ja|yuta}} increased.
The chaos of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}} severely reduced and scattered Okinawa's population, which further minimized the function of the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} today only exist in rural villages and at {{transliteration|ja|[[utaki]]}} sites.{{cn|date=June 2022}}
=={{transliteration|ja|Kikoe-ōgimi}}== {{main|Kikoe-ōgimi}}
The {{nihongo||聞得大君|kikoe-ōgimi}} (Okinawan: {{transliteration|ja|chifi-ufujin}}) served as the priestess for the Ryukyu Kingdom and the royal family. She conducted national ceremonies, oversaw all other {{transliteration|ja|noro}} priestesses, and maintained the royal hearth and the most sacred {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110-111.</ref> The inauguration ceremony, {{nihongo||御新下り|uarauri}}, of a new {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} was held at Seifa-utaki. The ceremony represented the holy marriage between Amamikyu and Shinerikyu. Religiously, the holy marriage gave the {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} spiritual power supported by {{nihongo||君手摩|[[kimitezuri]]}}. After she was inaugurated, she stayed in the position until her death. The position was abolished along with the kingdom in 1879; however, the last {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} continued her role until her death in 1944.
=={{transliteration|ja|Noro}} duties and responsibilities==
{{transliteration|ja|Noro}} were responsible for maintaining the village hearth and helping to establish new households.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32.</ref> They primarily conducted rituals and ceremonies for their respective village at a local {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}. Girls from each family in a village were assigned to be the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}'s assistant, although men could also be assistants for public ceremonies.<ref>Susan Sered, ''Women of the Sacred Grove: Divine Priestesses of Okinawa'' (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1999), 3-4, 18.</ref> {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} also communicated with and channeled ancestors and deities.
==Symbols==
{{transliteration|ja|Noro}} are most recognizable by their white robes and headdress, which symbolize spiritual purity.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 33.</ref> They also wear or carry beads and a {{transliteration|ja|[[magatama]]}} stone. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}}'s hearth includes three stones teepee-ed together; the stones always come from the shore.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32-33.</ref>
==Hierarchy==
{| class="wikitable" |+ Priestess hierarchy ! Title ! Description |- ! {{nihongo||聞得大君|{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi}}||extra=Okinawan: {{transliteration|ryu|chifi ufujin}}}} | Royal priestess, a blood relative of the king, residing in Shuri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Izena noro}} | Directly ruled {{ill|Izena, Okinawa|en|Izena, Okinawa|WD=Q981332|lt=Izena}} Magiri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Kudaka noro}} | Directly ruled {{ill|Kudaka Island|en|Kudaka Island|WD=Q4518835|lt=Kudaka Island}}. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|[[Mihara-oamu-shirare]]}} | Priestesses in charge of the three {{ill|Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q75020334|lt=districts}} of Kunigami, Nakagami, and Shimajiri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} | Village or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Magiri|Magiri]]}} priestess. |- ! Assistants | Typically a girl representing a family in a village or a {{transliteration|ja|noro}}-in-training, but can be a man for public ceremonies. |}

The first daughter became the first {{nihongo|royal priestess|聞得大君|{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi}}}} ({{transliteration|ryu|chifi-ufujin}}), and the second daughter became the first village priestess ({{transliteration|ja|noro}}).<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The god of fire gave a piece of fire from [[Ryūgū-jō]] to each {{transliteration|ja|noro}} to create a village hearth, from which each family in the village would take fire to maintain their own family hearths.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} maintained the royal hearth. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were charged with conducting official rituals and ceremonies for their respective village. The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} was charged with conducting rituals and ceremonies on behalf of the entire kingdom, and traveled with the king to [[Sefa-utaki]] to worship Amamikyu.
Upon taking the throne in 1469, King {{ill|Shō En|en|Shō En|WD=Q1074326|lt=Shō En}} made his sister the Chief {{transliteration|ja|noro}} of his home of {{ill|Izena, Okinawa|en|Izena, Okinawa|WD=Q981332|lt=Izena}}, and his daughter {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 103-104.</ref> During the reign of {{ill|Shō Shin|en|Shō Shin|WD=Q281765|lt=Shō Shin}} ({{reign|1477|1526}}), the priestess system was centralized under the {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}'s authority and a {{transliteration|ja|noro}} was assigned to every village in the kingdom.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} also had a new residence built near {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle}} so she could maintain the [[Sonohyan-utaki]].<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 111.</ref>
After the 1609 [[Invasion of Ryukyu|invasion by Satsuma]], Confucian thought entered the Ryukyuan government and began eroding the authority of the priestesses. Satsuma placed a demand on the Ryukyuan government that women were forbidden to own land; however, the government ignored this demand in respect to the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. [[Shō Shōken]], acting as Prime Minister of Ryukyu, convinced the king to stop or scale down a number of religious rituals and ceremonies in the name of saving money and minimizing aspects of Ryukyuan culture that might seem "backwards" to Satsuma, such as the king and {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}'s pilgrimage to Sefa-utaki. However, local village priestesses still retained influence and power until the end of the 19th century.
After Japan annexed the Ryukyu Kingdom in 1879, the Meiji government began attempts at absorbing the Ryukyuan religion into {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}. The priestesses and their shrines were co-opted by the government and registered. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were prohibited from being recognized as part of the Shinto priesthood and, unlike their Shinto counterparts, were not guaranteed protection by the state. Furthermore, the government abolished their salaries. While the government was unable to confiscate {{transliteration|ja|noro}} land, in 1910 their land were commuted to monetary stipends which were then limited in use to exclude personal expense, leaving the {{transliteration|ja|noro}} with no government income. Then-Governor of Okinawa Prefecture [[:en:Hibi Shigeaki|Hibi Shigeaki]] stated that this limitation on the use of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} land stipends was "for the maintenance of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} lands [...] for their eventual reclassification into [Shinto] shrines."<ref>Tze May Loo, ''Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan'' (New York: Lexington Books, 2014), 102.</ref> As the influence of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} declined, the popularity of {{transliteration|ja|yuta}} increased.
The chaos of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}} severely reduced and scattered Okinawa's population, which further minimized the function of the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} today only exist in rural villages and at {{transliteration|ja|[[utaki]]}} sites.{{cn|date=June 2022}}
=={{transliteration|ja|Kikoe-ōgimi}}== {{main|Kikoe-ōgimi}}
The {{nihongo||聞得大君|kikoe-ōgimi}} (Okinawan: {{transliteration|ja|chifi-ufujin}}) served as the priestess for the Ryukyu Kingdom and the royal family. She conducted national ceremonies, oversaw all other {{transliteration|ja|noro}} priestesses, and maintained the royal hearth and the most sacred {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110-111.</ref> The inauguration ceremony, {{nihongo||御新下り|uarauri}}, of a new {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} was held at Seifa-utaki. The ceremony represented the holy marriage between Amamikyu and Shinerikyu. Religiously, the holy marriage gave the {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} spiritual power supported by {{nihongo||君手摩|[[kimitezuri]]}}. After she was inaugurated, she stayed in the position until her death. The position was abolished along with the kingdom in 1879; however, the last {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} continued her role until her death in 1944.
=={{transliteration|ja|Noro}} duties and responsibilities==
{{transliteration|ja|Noro}} were responsible for maintaining the village hearth and helping to establish new households.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32.</ref> They primarily conducted rituals and ceremonies for their respective village at a local {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}. Girls from each family in a village were assigned to be the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}'s assistant, although men could also be assistants for public ceremonies.<ref>Susan Sered, ''Women of the Sacred Grove: Divine Priestesses of Okinawa'' (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1999), 3-4, 18.</ref> {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} also communicated with and channeled ancestors and deities.
==Symbols==
{{transliteration|ja|Noro}} are most recognizable by their white robes and headdress, which symbolize spiritual purity.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 33.</ref> They also wear or carry beads and a {{transliteration|ja|[[magatama]]}} stone. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}}'s hearth includes three stones teepee-ed together; the stones always come from the shore.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32-33.</ref>
==Hierarchy==
{| class="wikitable" |+ Priestess hierarchy ! Title ! Description |- ! {{nihongo||聞得大君|{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi}}||extra=Okinawan: {{transliteration|ryu|chifi ufujin}}}} | Royal priestess, a blood relative of the king, residing in Shuri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Izena noro}} | Directly ruled {{ill|Izena, Okinawa|en|Izena, Okinawa|WD=Q981332|lt=Izena}} Magiri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Kudaka noro}} | Directly ruled {{ill|Kudaka Island|en|Kudaka Island|WD=Q4518835|lt=Kudaka Island}}. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|[[Mihara-oamu-shirare]]}} | Priestesses in charge of the three {{ill|Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q75020334|lt=districts}} of Kunigami, Nakagami, and Shimajiri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} | Village or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Magiri|Magiri]]}} priestess. |- ! Assistants | Typically a girl representing a family in a village or a {{transliteration|ja|noro}}-in-training, but can be a man for public ceremonies. |}

They have existed since at least the beginning of the [[History of the Ryukyu Islands#Gusuku period|Gusuku period]] (late 12th century) and continue to perform rituals even today. They are distinct from {{transliteration|ryu|{{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=yuta}}}} (psychics), but are classified as {{transliteration|ryu|kaminchu}} ("godly people").

Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The god of fire gave a piece of fire from [[Ryūgū-jō]] to each {{transliteration|ja|noro}} to create a village hearth, from which each family in the village would take fire to maintain their own family hearths.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} maintained the royal hearth. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} also had a new residence built near {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle}} so she could maintain the [[Sonohyan-utaki]].<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 111.</ref>

Satsuma placed a demand on the Ryukyuan government that women were forbidden to own land; however, the government ignored this demand in respect to the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. [[Shō Shōken]], acting as Prime Minister of Ryukyu, convinced the king to stop or scale down a number of religious rituals and ceremonies in the name of saving money and minimizing aspects of Ryukyuan culture that might seem "backwards" to Satsuma, such as the king and {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}'s pilgrimage to Sefa-utaki. Furthermore, the government abolished their salaries. Then-Governor of Okinawa Prefecture [[:en:Hibi Shigeaki|Hibi Shigeaki]] stated that this limitation on the use of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} land stipends was "for the maintenance of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} lands [...] for their eventual reclassification into [Shinto] shrines."<ref>Tze May Loo, ''Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan'' (New York: Lexington Books, 2014), 102.</ref> As the influence of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} declined, the popularity of {{transliteration|ja|yuta}} increased.
The chaos of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}} severely reduced and scattered Okinawa's population, which further minimized the function of the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} today only exist in rural villages and at {{transliteration|ja|[[utaki]]}} sites.{{cn|date=June 2022}}

Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32-33.</ref>

=={{transliteration|ja|Kikoe-ōgimi}}== {{main|Kikoe-ōgimi}}
The {{nihongo||聞得大君|kikoe-ōgimi}} (Okinawan: {{transliteration|ja|chifi-ufujin}}) served as the priestess for the Ryukyu Kingdom and the royal family. She conducted national ceremonies, oversaw all other {{transliteration|ja|noro}} priestesses, and maintained the royal hearth and the most sacred {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110-111.</ref> The inauguration ceremony, {{nihongo||御新下り|uarauri}}, of a new {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} was held at Seifa-utaki. The ceremony represented the holy marriage between Amamikyu and Shinerikyu. Religiously, the holy marriage gave the {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} spiritual power supported by {{nihongo||君手摩|[[kimitezuri]]}}. After she was inaugurated, she stayed in the position until her death. The position was abolished along with the kingdom in 1879; however, the last {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} continued her role until her death in 1944.
=={{transliteration|ja|Noro}} duties and responsibilities==
{{transliteration|ja|Noro}} were responsible for maintaining the village hearth and helping to establish new households.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32.</ref> They primarily conducted rituals and ceremonies for their respective village at a local {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}. Girls from each family in a village were assigned to be the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}'s assistant, although men could also be assistants for public ceremonies.<ref>Susan Sered, ''Women of the Sacred Grove: Divine Priestesses of Okinawa'' (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1999), 3-4, 18.</ref> {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} also communicated with and channeled ancestors and deities.
==Symbols==
{{transliteration|ja|Noro}} are most recognizable by their white robes and headdress, which symbolize spiritual purity.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 33.</ref> They also wear or carry beads and a {{transliteration|ja|[[magatama]]}} stone. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}}'s hearth includes three stones teepee-ed together; the stones always come from the shore.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32-33.</ref>
==Hierarchy==

Description |- ! {{nihongo||聞得大君|{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}}||extra=Okinawan: {{transliteration|ryu|chifi ufujin}}}} | Royal priestess, a blood relative of the king, residing in Shuri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Izena noro}} | Directly ruled {{ill|Izena, Okinawa|en|Izena, Okinawa|WD=Q981332|lt=Izena|1=Izena, Okinawa}} Magiri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Kudaka noro}} | Directly ruled {{ill|Kudaka Island|en|Kudaka Island|WD=Q4518835|lt=Kudaka Island|1=Kudaka Island}}. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|[[Mihara-oamu-shirare]]}} | Priestesses in charge of the three {{ill|Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q75020334|lt=districts|1=Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom}} of Kunigami, Nakagami, and Shimajiri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} | Village or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Magiri|Magiri]]}} priestess. |- ! Assistants | Typically a girl representing a family in a village or a {{transliteration|ja|noro}}-in-training, but can be a man for public ceremonies. |}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Noro (priestess)]][[fr:Noro (prêtresse)]][[ja:ノロ]][[ko:노로]][[pt:Noro]][[zh:祝女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Noro (priestess)]][[fr:Noro (prêtresse)]][[ja:ノロ]][[ko:노로]][[pt:Noro]][[zh:祝女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Noro (priestess)]][[fr:Noro (prêtresse)]][[ja:ノロ]][[ko:노로]][[pt:Noro]][[zh:祝女]]
{{Short description|Priestess of the Ryukyuan religion}} {{italic title}} [[File:Noro.JPG|thumb|right|A {{transliteration|ja|noro}} priestess in traditional clothes]] {{transliteration|ja|'''Noro'''}} ({{lang|ja|祝女}}, sometimes {{lang|ja|神女}} or {{lang|ja|巫女}}) ({{langx|ryu|ヌール|nuuru}})<ref>[http://ryukyu-lang.lib.u-ryukyu.ac.jp/srnh/details.php?ID=SN40789 ヌール] 首里・那覇方言データベース</ref> are {{ill|Priest|en|Priest|WD=Q42603|lt=Priest|1=Priest}}esses of the [[Ryukyuan religion]] at [[Utaki]]. They have existed since at least the beginning of the [[History of the Ryukyu Islands#Gusuku period|Gusuku period]] (late 12th century) and continue to perform rituals even today. They are distinct from {{transliteration|ryu|{{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=yuta|1=Yuta (shaman)}}}} (psychics), but are classified as {{transliteration|ryu|kaminchu}} ("godly people").
==History== According to the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Chūzan Seikan|en|Chūzan Seikan|WD=Q8015936|lt=Chūzan Seikan|1=Chūzan Seikan}}}} and {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Chūzan Seifu|en|Chūzan Seifu|WD=Q10875195|lt=Chūzan Seifu|1=Chūzan Seifu}}}}, the first {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were the daughters of Tentei-shi, who was a descendant of the creation goddess, [[Amamikyu]]. The first daughter became the first {{nihongo|royal priestess|聞得大君|{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}}}} ({{transliteration|ryu|chifi-ufujin}}), and the second daughter became the first village priestess ({{transliteration|ja|noro}}).<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The god of fire gave a piece of fire from [[Ryūgū-jō]] to each {{transliteration|ja|noro}} to create a village hearth, from which each family in the village would take fire to maintain their own family hearths.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} maintained the royal hearth. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were charged with conducting official rituals and ceremonies for their respective village. The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} was charged with conducting rituals and ceremonies on behalf of the entire kingdom, and traveled with the king to [[Sefa-utaki]] to worship Amamikyu.
Upon taking the throne in 1469, King {{ill|Shō En|en|Shō En|WD=Q1074326|lt=Shō En|1=Shō En}} made his sister the Chief {{transliteration|ja|noro}} of his home of {{ill|Izena, Okinawa|en|Izena, Okinawa|WD=Q981332|lt=Izena|1=Izena, Okinawa}}, and his daughter {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 103-104.</ref> During the reign of {{ill|Shō Shin|en|Shō Shin|WD=Q281765|lt=Shō Shin|1=Shō Shin}} ({{reign|1477|1526}}), the priestess system was centralized under the {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}'s authority and a {{transliteration|ja|noro}} was assigned to every village in the kingdom.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} also had a new residence built near {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle|1=Shuri Castle}} so she could maintain the [[Sonohyan-utaki]].<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 111.</ref>
After the 1609 [[Invasion of Ryukyu|invasion by Satsuma]], Confucian thought entered the Ryukyuan government and began eroding the authority of the priestesses. Satsuma placed a demand on the Ryukyuan government that women were forbidden to own land; however, the government ignored this demand in respect to the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. [[Shō Shōken]], acting as Prime Minister of Ryukyu, convinced the king to stop or scale down a number of religious rituals and ceremonies in the name of saving money and minimizing aspects of Ryukyuan culture that might seem "backwards" to Satsuma, such as the king and {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}'s pilgrimage to Sefa-utaki. However, local village priestesses still retained influence and power until the end of the 19th century.
After Japan annexed the Ryukyu Kingdom in 1879, the Meiji government began attempts at absorbing the Ryukyuan religion into {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}. The priestesses and their shrines were co-opted by the government and registered. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were prohibited from being recognized as part of the Shinto priesthood and, unlike their Shinto counterparts, were not guaranteed protection by the state. Furthermore, the government abolished their salaries. While the government was unable to confiscate {{transliteration|ja|noro}} land, in 1910 their land were commuted to monetary stipends which were then limited in use to exclude personal expense, leaving the {{transliteration|ja|noro}} with no government income. Then-Governor of Okinawa Prefecture [[:en:Hibi Shigeaki|Hibi Shigeaki]] stated that this limitation on the use of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} land stipends was "for the maintenance of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} lands [...] for their eventual reclassification into [Shinto] shrines."<ref>Tze May Loo, ''Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan'' (New York: Lexington Books, 2014), 102.</ref> As the influence of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} declined, the popularity of {{transliteration|ja|yuta}} increased.
The chaos of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} severely reduced and scattered Okinawa's population, which further minimized the function of the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} today only exist in rural villages and at {{transliteration|ja|[[utaki]]}} sites.{{cn|date=June 2022}}
=={{transliteration|ja|Kikoe-ōgimi}}== {{main|Kikoe-ōgimi}}
The {{nihongo||聞得大君|kikoe-ōgimi}} (Okinawan: {{transliteration|ja|chifi-ufujin}}) served as the priestess for the Ryukyu Kingdom and the royal family. She conducted national ceremonies, oversaw all other {{transliteration|ja|noro}} priestesses, and maintained the royal hearth and the most sacred {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110-111.</ref> The inauguration ceremony, {{nihongo||御新下り|uarauri}}, of a new {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} was held at Seifa-utaki. The ceremony represented the holy marriage between Amamikyu and Shinerikyu. Religiously, the holy marriage gave the {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} spiritual power supported by {{nihongo||君手摩|[[kimitezuri]]}}. After she was inaugurated, she stayed in the position until her death. The position was abolished along with the kingdom in 1879; however, the last {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} continued her role until her death in 1944.
=={{transliteration|ja|Noro}} duties and responsibilities==
{{transliteration|ja|Noro}} were responsible for maintaining the village hearth and helping to establish new households.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32.</ref> They primarily conducted rituals and ceremonies for their respective village at a local {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}. Girls from each family in a village were assigned to be the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}'s assistant, although men could also be assistants for public ceremonies.<ref>Susan Sered, ''Women of the Sacred Grove: Divine Priestesses of Okinawa'' (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1999), 3-4, 18.</ref> {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} also communicated with and channeled ancestors and deities.
==Symbols==
{{transliteration|ja|Noro}} are most recognizable by their white robes and headdress, which symbolize spiritual purity.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 33.</ref> They also wear or carry beads and a {{transliteration|ja|[[magatama]]}} stone. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}}'s hearth includes three stones teepee-ed together; the stones always come from the shore.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32-33.</ref>
==Hierarchy==

Description |- ! {{nihongo||聞得大君|{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}}||extra=Okinawan: {{transliteration|ryu|chifi ufujin}}}} | Royal priestess, a blood relative of the king, residing in Shuri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Izena noro}} | Directly ruled {{ill|Izena, Okinawa|en|Izena, Okinawa|WD=Q981332|lt=Izena|1=Izena, Okinawa}} Magiri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Kudaka noro}} | Directly ruled {{ill|Kudaka Island|en|Kudaka Island|WD=Q4518835|lt=Kudaka Island|1=Kudaka Island}}. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|[[Mihara-oamu-shirare]]}} | Priestesses in charge of the three {{ill|Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q75020334|lt=districts|1=Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom}} of Kunigami, Nakagami, and Shimajiri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} | Village or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Magiri|Magiri]]}} priestess. |- ! Assistants | Typically a girl representing a family in a village or a {{transliteration|ja|noro}}-in-training, but can be a man for public ceremonies. |}

{{Short description|Priestess of the Ryukyuan religion}} {{italic title}} [[File:Noro.JPG|thumb|right|A {{transliteration|ja|noro}} priestess in traditional clothes]] {{transliteration|ja|'''Noro'''}} ({{lang|ja|祝女}}, sometimes {{lang|ja|神女}} or {{lang|ja|巫女}}) ({{langx|ryu|ヌール|nuuru}})<ref>[http://ryukyu-lang.lib.u-ryukyu.ac.jp/srnh/details.php?ID=SN40789 ヌール] 首里・那覇方言データベース</ref> are {{ill|Priest|en|Priest|WD=Q42603|lt=Priest|1=Priest}}esses of the [[Ryukyuan religion]] at [[Utaki]]. They have existed since at least the beginning of the [[History of the Ryukyu Islands#Gusuku period|Gusuku period]] (late 12th century) and continue to perform rituals even today. They are distinct from {{transliteration|ryu|{{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=yuta|1=Yuta (shaman)}}}} (psychics), but are classified as {{transliteration|ryu|kaminchu}} ("godly people").
==History== According to the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Chūzan Seikan|en|Chūzan Seikan|WD=Q8015936|lt=Chūzan Seikan|1=Chūzan Seikan}}}} and {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Chūzan Seifu|en|Chūzan Seifu|WD=Q10875195|lt=Chūzan Seifu|1=Chūzan Seifu}}}}, the first {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were the daughters of Tentei-shi, who was a descendant of the creation goddess, [[Amamikyu]]. The first daughter became the first {{nihongo|royal priestess|聞得大君|{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}}}} ({{transliteration|ryu|chifi-ufujin}}), and the second daughter became the first village priestess ({{transliteration|ja|noro}}).<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The god of fire gave a piece of fire from [[Ryūgū-jō]] to each {{transliteration|ja|noro}} to create a village hearth, from which each family in the village would take fire to maintain their own family hearths.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} maintained the royal hearth. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were charged with conducting official rituals and ceremonies for their respective village. The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} was charged with conducting rituals and ceremonies on behalf of the entire kingdom, and traveled with the king to [[Sefa-utaki]] to worship Amamikyu.
Upon taking the throne in 1469, King {{ill|Shō En|en|Shō En|WD=Q1074326|lt=Shō En|1=Shō En}} made his sister the Chief {{transliteration|ja|noro}} of his home of {{ill|Izena, Okinawa|en|Izena, Okinawa|WD=Q981332|lt=Izena|1=Izena, Okinawa}}, and his daughter {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 103-104.</ref> During the reign of {{ill|Shō Shin|en|Shō Shin|WD=Q281765|lt=Shō Shin|1=Shō Shin}} ({{reign|1477|1526}}), the priestess system was centralized under the {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}'s authority and a {{transliteration|ja|noro}} was assigned to every village in the kingdom.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} also had a new residence built near {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle|1=Shuri Castle}} so she could maintain the [[Sonohyan-utaki]].<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 111.</ref>
After the 1609 [[Invasion of Ryukyu|invasion by Satsuma]], Confucian thought entered the Ryukyuan government and began eroding the authority of the priestesses. Satsuma placed a demand on the Ryukyuan government that women were forbidden to own land; however, the government ignored this demand in respect to the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. [[Shō Shōken]], acting as Prime Minister of Ryukyu, convinced the king to stop or scale down a number of religious rituals and ceremonies in the name of saving money and minimizing aspects of Ryukyuan culture that might seem "backwards" to Satsuma, such as the king and {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}'s pilgrimage to Sefa-utaki. However, local village priestesses still retained influence and power until the end of the 19th century.
After Japan annexed the Ryukyu Kingdom in 1879, the Meiji government began attempts at absorbing the Ryukyuan religion into {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}. The priestesses and their shrines were co-opted by the government and registered. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were prohibited from being recognized as part of the Shinto priesthood and, unlike their Shinto counterparts, were not guaranteed protection by the state. Furthermore, the government abolished their salaries. While the government was unable to confiscate {{transliteration|ja|noro}} land, in 1910 their land were commuted to monetary stipends which were then limited in use to exclude personal expense, leaving the {{transliteration|ja|noro}} with no government income. Then-Governor of Okinawa Prefecture [[:en:Hibi Shigeaki|Hibi Shigeaki]] stated that this limitation on the use of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} land stipends was "for the maintenance of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} lands [...] for their eventual reclassification into [Shinto] shrines."<ref>Tze May Loo, ''Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan'' (New York: Lexington Books, 2014), 102.</ref> As the influence of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} declined, the popularity of {{transliteration|ja|yuta}} increased.
The chaos of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} severely reduced and scattered Okinawa's population, which further minimized the function of the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} today only exist in rural villages and at {{transliteration|ja|[[utaki]]}} sites.{{cn|date=June 2022}}

She conducted national ceremonies, oversaw all other {{transliteration|ja|noro}} priestesses, and maintained the royal hearth and the most sacred {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110-111.</ref> The inauguration ceremony, {{nihongo||御新下り|uarauri}}, of a new {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} was held at Seifa-utaki. The ceremony represented the holy marriage between Amamikyu and Shinerikyu. Religiously, the holy marriage gave the {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} spiritual power supported by {{nihongo||君手摩|[[kimitezuri]]}}. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32.</ref> They primarily conducted rituals and ceremonies for their respective village at a local {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}. Tuttle Company, 1958), 33.</ref> They also wear or carry beads and a {{transliteration|ja|[[magatama]]}} stone. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}}'s hearth includes three stones teepee-ed together; the stones always come from the shore.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32-33.</ref>

{{Short description|Priestess of the Ryukyuan religion}} {{italic title}} [[File:Noro.JPG|thumb|right|A {{transliteration|ja|noro}} priestess in traditional clothes]] {{transliteration|ja|'''Noro'''}} ({{lang|ja|祝女}}, sometimes {{lang|ja|神女}} or {{lang|ja|巫女}}) ({{langx|ryu|ヌール|nuuru}})<ref>[http://ryukyu-lang.lib.u-ryukyu.ac.jp/srnh/details.php?ID=SN40789 ヌール] 首里・那覇方言データベース</ref> are {{ill|Priest|en|Priest|WD=Q42603|lt=Priest|1=Priest}}esses of the [[Ryukyuan religion]] at [[Utaki]]. They have existed since at least the beginning of the [[History of the Ryukyu Islands#Gusuku period|Gusuku period]] (late 12th century) and continue to perform rituals even today. They are distinct from {{transliteration|ryu|{{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|WD=Q11345595|lt=yuta|1=Yuta (shaman)}}}} (psychics), but are classified as {{transliteration|ryu|kaminchu}} ("godly people").
==History== According to the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Chūzan Seikan|en|Chūzan Seikan|WD=Q8015936|lt=Chūzan Seikan|1=Chūzan Seikan}}}} and {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Chūzan Seifu|en|Chūzan Seifu|WD=Q10875195|lt=Chūzan Seifu|1=Chūzan Seifu}}}}, the first {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were the daughters of Tentei-shi, who was a descendant of the creation goddess, [[Amamikyu]]. The first daughter became the first {{nihongo|royal priestess|聞得大君|{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}}}} ({{transliteration|ryu|chifi-ufujin}}), and the second daughter became the first village priestess ({{transliteration|ja|noro}}).<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The god of fire gave a piece of fire from [[Ryūgū-jō]] to each {{transliteration|ja|noro}} to create a village hearth, from which each family in the village would take fire to maintain their own family hearths.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 36.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} maintained the royal hearth. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were charged with conducting official rituals and ceremonies for their respective village. The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} was charged with conducting rituals and ceremonies on behalf of the entire kingdom, and traveled with the king to [[Sefa-utaki]] to worship Amamikyu.
Upon taking the throne in 1469, King {{ill|Shō En|en|Shō En|WD=Q1074326|lt=Shō En|1=Shō En}} made his sister the Chief {{transliteration|ja|noro}} of his home of {{ill|Izena, Okinawa|en|Izena, Okinawa|WD=Q981332|lt=Izena|1=Izena, Okinawa}}, and his daughter {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 103-104.</ref> During the reign of {{ill|Shō Shin|en|Shō Shin|WD=Q281765|lt=Shō Shin|1=Shō Shin}} ({{reign|1477|1526}}), the priestess system was centralized under the {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}'s authority and a {{transliteration|ja|noro}} was assigned to every village in the kingdom.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110.</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} also had a new residence built near {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle|1=Shuri Castle}} so she could maintain the [[Sonohyan-utaki]].<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 111.</ref>
After the 1609 [[Invasion of Ryukyu|invasion by Satsuma]], Confucian thought entered the Ryukyuan government and began eroding the authority of the priestesses. Satsuma placed a demand on the Ryukyuan government that women were forbidden to own land; however, the government ignored this demand in respect to the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. [[Shō Shōken]], acting as Prime Minister of Ryukyu, convinced the king to stop or scale down a number of religious rituals and ceremonies in the name of saving money and minimizing aspects of Ryukyuan culture that might seem "backwards" to Satsuma, such as the king and {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}}'s pilgrimage to Sefa-utaki. However, local village priestesses still retained influence and power until the end of the 19th century.
After Japan annexed the Ryukyu Kingdom in 1879, the Meiji government began attempts at absorbing the Ryukyuan religion into {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}. The priestesses and their shrines were co-opted by the government and registered. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}} were prohibited from being recognized as part of the Shinto priesthood and, unlike their Shinto counterparts, were not guaranteed protection by the state. Furthermore, the government abolished their salaries. While the government was unable to confiscate {{transliteration|ja|noro}} land, in 1910 their land were commuted to monetary stipends which were then limited in use to exclude personal expense, leaving the {{transliteration|ja|noro}} with no government income. Then-Governor of Okinawa Prefecture [[:en:Hibi Shigeaki|Hibi Shigeaki]] stated that this limitation on the use of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} land stipends was "for the maintenance of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} lands [...] for their eventual reclassification into [Shinto] shrines."<ref>Tze May Loo, ''Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan'' (New York: Lexington Books, 2014), 102.</ref> As the influence of {{transliteration|ja|noro}} declined, the popularity of {{transliteration|ja|yuta}} increased.
The chaos of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} severely reduced and scattered Okinawa's population, which further minimized the function of the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}. {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} today only exist in rural villages and at {{transliteration|ja|[[utaki]]}} sites.{{cn|date=June 2022}}
=={{transliteration|ja|Kikoe-ōgimi}}== {{main|Kikoe-ōgimi}}
The {{nihongo||聞得大君|kikoe-ōgimi}} (Okinawan: {{transliteration|ja|chifi-ufujin}}) served as the priestess for the Ryukyu Kingdom and the royal family. She conducted national ceremonies, oversaw all other {{transliteration|ja|noro}} priestesses, and maintained the royal hearth and the most sacred {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 110-111.</ref> The inauguration ceremony, {{nihongo||御新下り|uarauri}}, of a new {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} was held at Seifa-utaki. The ceremony represented the holy marriage between Amamikyu and Shinerikyu. Religiously, the holy marriage gave the {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} spiritual power supported by {{nihongo||君手摩|[[kimitezuri]]}}. After she was inaugurated, she stayed in the position until her death. The position was abolished along with the kingdom in 1879; however, the last {{transliteration|ja|kikoe-ōgimi}} continued her role until her death in 1944.
=={{transliteration|ja|Noro}} duties and responsibilities==
{{transliteration|ja|Noro}} were responsible for maintaining the village hearth and helping to establish new households.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32.</ref> They primarily conducted rituals and ceremonies for their respective village at a local {{transliteration|ja|utaki}}. Girls from each family in a village were assigned to be the {{transliteration|ja|noro}}'s assistant, although men could also be assistants for public ceremonies.<ref>Susan Sered, ''Women of the Sacred Grove: Divine Priestesses of Okinawa'' (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1999), 3-4, 18.</ref> {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} also communicated with and channeled ancestors and deities.
==Symbols==
{{transliteration|ja|Noro}} are most recognizable by their white robes and headdress, which symbolize spiritual purity.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 33.</ref> They also wear or carry beads and a {{transliteration|ja|[[magatama]]}} stone. The {{transliteration|ja|noro}}'s hearth includes three stones teepee-ed together; the stones always come from the shore.<ref>George H. Kerr, ''Okinawa: History of an Island People'' (Tokyo: Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1958), 32-33.</ref>

Description |- ! {{nihongo||聞得大君|{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}}||extra=Okinawan: {{transliteration|ryu|chifi ufujin}}}} | Royal priestess, a blood relative of the king, residing in Shuri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Izena noro}} | Directly ruled {{ill|Izena, Okinawa|en|Izena, Okinawa|WD=Q981332|lt=Izena|1=Izena, Okinawa}} Magiri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Kudaka noro}} | Directly ruled {{ill|Kudaka Island|en|Kudaka Island|WD=Q4518835|lt=Kudaka Island|1=Kudaka Island}}. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|[[Mihara-oamu-shirare]]}} | Priestesses in charge of the three {{ill|Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q75020334|lt=districts|1=Administrative divisions of the Ryukyu Kingdom}} of Kunigami, Nakagami, and Shimajiri. |- ! {{transliteration|ja|Noro}} | Village or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Magiri|Magiri]]}} priestess. |- ! Assistants | Typically a girl representing a family in a village or a {{transliteration|ja|noro}}-in-training, but can be a man for public ceremonies. |}
